questions.question:
It would take one machine 4 hours to complete a production order and another machine 2 hours to complete the same order. If both machines work simultaneously at their respective constant rates, the time taken to complete the same order is ________ hours.

A. 2/3
B. 3/4
C. 4/3
D. 7/3
questions.to_find: Time taken by both machines working simultaneously to complete one production order

understand.plain: Two machines can each independently complete the same production order, but at different speeds. Machine 1 needs 4 hours alone while Machine 2 needs only 2 hours. When both run at the same time, their rates add together, so the job gets done faster than either machine could alone. We need to find this combined completion time.

keywords.explain: Fraction of the total job completed per unit time. If a machine finishes the job in T hours, its rate is 1/T jobs per hour.
keywords.example: Machine taking 4 hours has rate 1/4 of the order per hour.

keywords.explain: When two workers (or machines) work simultaneously, their individual rates add up.
keywords.example: Rates 1/4 and 1/2 together give 1/4 + 1/2 = 3/4 orders per hour.

keywords.explain: If the combined rate is R orders per hour, the time to complete one order is 1/R hours.

understand.visual_alt: Two machines contributing partial work each hour to complete one full order together.

given.aim: Find the combined time for both machines to complete the production order together

terms.meaning: 4 hours to finish the order alone
terms.example: In 1 hour it completes 1/4 of the order
terms.connects: Contributes rate 1/4 to the combined effort

terms.meaning: 2 hours to finish the order alone
terms.example: In 1 hour it completes 1/2 of the order
terms.connects: Contributes rate 1/2 to the combined effort

terms.meaning: When working simultaneously, rates add: R_total = R_1 + R_2

given.plan: Find each machine's hourly rate (1/time), add them to get combined rate, then take reciprocal for combined time.

steps.title: Find individual rates
steps.apply: Rate_1 = 1/4 orders per hour (Machine 1, takes 4 hrs). Rate_2 = 1/2 orders per hour (Machine 2, takes 2 hrs).
steps.note: Rate is the fraction of the whole job done in one hour.

steps.title: Add rates to get combined rate
steps.note: This works because each machine independently completes its fraction of the order each hour.

steps.title: Find combined time
steps.note: 4/3 hours is approximately 1 hour 20 minutes, which is less than either machine alone.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is Rate = 1/T and combined time = 1/(sum of rates), which always gives a time less than the faster individual.

questions.question:
It takes two hours for a person X to mow the lawn. Y can mow the same lawn in four hours. How long (in minutes) will it take X and Y, if they work together to mow the lawn?

A. 60
B. 80
C. 90
D. 120
questions.to_find: Time in minutes for X and Y working together to mow the entire lawn

understand.plain: Person X mows the entire lawn in 2 hours, and Person Y does the same job in 4 hours. Working side by side their per-hour contributions add up. We must find the combined time and convert from hours to minutes by multiplying by 60.

keywords.explain: Fraction of the lawn one person can mow in one hour. X mows 1/2 per hour; Y mows 1/4 per hour.
keywords.example: In 1 hour X finishes half the lawn, Y finishes one quarter.

keywords.explain: Multiply hours by 60 to convert to minutes since 1 hour = 60 minutes.
Two workers doing the same task at the same time contribute independently at their own rates.
keywords.example: X and Y together mow at rate 1/2 + 1/4 = 3/4 of the lawn per hour.

understand.visual_alt: X and Y each mowing different strips of the same lawn at the same time.

given.aim: Find combined mowing time in minutes for X and Y working together

terms.meaning: X completes the whole lawn in 2 hours, so rate = 1/2 lawn per hour
terms.example: After 1 hour, X has mowed exactly half the lawn
terms.connects: Contributes 1/2 to the combined hourly rate

terms.meaning: Y completes the whole lawn in 4 hours, so rate = 1/4 lawn per hour
terms.example: After 1 hour, Y has mowed exactly one quarter of the lawn
terms.connects: Contributes 1/4 to the combined hourly rate

terms.meaning: 1 hour = 60 minutes; multiply fractional hours by 60 to get minutes
terms.connects: Final step converts the hours answer to the required minutes

given.plan: Compute combined rate = 1/2 + 1/4, invert to get hours, then multiply by 60 for minutes.

steps.title: Compute individual rates
steps.note: Each rate is the reciprocal of that person's solo completion time.

steps.title: Combined rate when working together
steps.note: Their efforts are additive because they work on different parts simultaneously.

steps.title: Time in hours
steps.note: 4/3 is between 1 and 2 hours, which is sensible since X alone takes 2 hours.

steps.title: Convert to minutes
steps.note: The question specifically asks for the answer in minutes.

questions.formula_note: The combined time formula 1/(1/a + 1/b) = ab/(a+b) is a useful shortcut: here it gives 2x4/(2+4) = 8/6 = 4/3 hours = 80 minutes.

questions.question:
Two pipes P and Q can fill a tank in 9 hours and 6 hours respectively, while a third pipe R can empty the tank in 12 hours. Initially, P and R are open for 6 hours. Then P is closed and Q is opened. After 6 more hours R is closed. The total time taken to fill the tank (in hours) is ____

A. 13.50
B. 14.00
C. 15.50
D. 14.50
questions.to_find: Total time in hours to completely fill the tank given the three-phase pipe operation

understand.plain: A tank has two inlet pipes P and Q and one outlet pipe R. The filling happens in three distinct phases: first P and R run together, then R continues while Q replaces P, and finally only Q runs alone after R closes. We track the cumulative fraction of the tank filled after each phase and compute when the total reaches 1 (full).

keywords.explain: When inlets and outlets run simultaneously, effective rate = sum of inlet rates minus sum of outlet rates.
keywords.example: P (fills at 1/9) and R (empties at 1/12) together: net = 1/9 - 1/12 = 1/36 per hour.

keywords.explain: Total fraction filled = sum of (rate x time) for each phase, ensuring we track correctly across phase changes.
keywords.example: Phase 1 fills 1/6; Phase 2 fills 1/2; together 2/3 of the tank is filled before Phase 3.

keywords.explain: When pipe configurations change at specific times, split the problem into phases and handle each separately.
keywords.example: Phase 3 starts with 1/3 of tank remaining; Q alone at 1/6 per hour takes 2 more hours.

class='mut sub'>t=6 to 12 hrs</text><text x='259' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='ok tag'>Q open (fills)</text><text x='259' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='no tag'>R open (empties)</text><text x='259' y='116' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Net rate=1/12/hr</text><text x='259' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Work = 1/2</text><rect x='352' y='30' width='152' height='140' text-anchor='middle' class='ok tag'>Q open (fills)</text><text x='428' y='94' text-anchor='middle' class='mut tag'>R closed</text><text x='428' y='116' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>Rate = hrs</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three-phase timeline showing which pipes are open and the fraction of tank filled in each phase.

given.aim: Find total hours to fill tank using P, Q (inlets) and R (outlet) in a three-phase sequence

terms.meaning: P fills the tank alone in 9 hours, contributing 1/9 of the tank per hour
terms.example: In 6 hours, P alone would fill 6/9 = 2/3 of the tank
terms.connects: Used in Phase 1 along with R; closed at t=6 hrs

terms.meaning: Q fills the tank alone in 6 hours, contributing 1/6 of the tank per hour
terms.example: In 6 hours, Q alone would fill 6/6 = 1 full tank
terms.connects: Opened at t=6 hrs in Phase 2; runs alone in Phase 3 after R closes

terms.meaning: R empties the full tank in 12 hours, draining 1/12 of the tank per hour
terms.example: While running, R subtracts 1/12 per hour from the filling progress
terms.connects: Open during Phase 1 and Phase 2; closed at t=12 hrs to allow Phase 3

given.plan: Calculate net work done in each phase (rate x time), sum to find fraction filled after Phase 2, then compute Phase 3 duration for Q alone to fill the remainder.

steps.title: Phase 1: P and R open for 6 hours
steps.note: R reduces the effective filling rate significantly; P alone would fill 2/3 in 6 hours but the net is only 1/6.

steps.title: Phase 2: P closed, Q opened; Q and R open for 6 more hours
steps.note: Q is faster than P, so more work gets done in Phase 2 despite R still running.

steps.title: Cumulative fill after 12 hours
steps.note: After 12 hours, only one-third of the tank still needs filling.

steps.title: Phase 3: R closed, Q alone fills remaining 1/3
steps.note: With R closed, Q works at full rate 1/6 per hour, finishing the last third in 2 hours.

steps.title: Total time
steps.note: The three phases account for 1/6 + 1/2 + 1/3 = 1 full tank, confirming the answer.

questions.formula_note: Phase-wise analysis with net rates (inlet minus outlet) is essential when drainage pipes run simultaneously with filling pipes.

formulas.description: Individual work rate: if a worker completes one full job in T time units, their rate is 1/T jobs per unit time.
Combined work rate when n workers work simultaneously equals the sum of their individual rates.
Time to complete one unit of work given a combined rate R_combined; equivalently T = ab/(a+b) for two workers with times a and b.
Unit conversion from hours to minutes; multiply fractional hours by 60.
Effective filling rate when both inlet and outlet pipes operate simultaneously.

formulas.formula: W_total = sum over phases of (net_rate_i x t_i)
formulas.description: Total fraction of work completed across multiple phases with different pipe or worker configurations.
Total elapsed time equals the sum of durations of all sequential phases.
